<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: semantic-pages-context-cells-and-certificates
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/semantic-pages-context-cells-and-certificates.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/semantic-pages-context-cells-and-certificates.qmd@4527feafe
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Semantic Pages, Context Cells, and Certificates

The Virtual Context ABI gets context into the working surface. This chapter
asks what happens next.

Once a packet has been materialized, the system still has to know what each
piece of context is allowed to mean. A paragraph can be a raw source excerpt,
a lossy summary, a redacted instruction, a planning hint, a claim cell, a
contradiction pair, or a reader paraphrase. Those objects can all be made of
words, but they do not carry the same authority. Treating them as the same
kind of context is how a useful summary becomes false evidence.

Semantic pages and context cells are the book's answer to that problem. They
make context into typed units. Certificates make the units honest. A
certificate says where the cell came from, what was omitted, what was lost in
translation or compression, which authority ceiling survived, which uses are
allowed, whether the cell is current, and what residual risk remains.

The reader throughline is simple: a summary is not reusable context until it
carries its loss, provenance, authority, permitted use, and revocation state
with it.

## Problem

Compiled context needs typed semantic units and proof-carrying summaries that
preserve provenance and loss contracts.

The ABI can say that a context packet was requested, resolved, materialized,
leased, or faulted. That still does not tell the next layer what each unit
inside the packet is. A worker may receive a small planning brief. A verifier
may need the exact source. A claim ledger may need citation links and
contradiction history. An executor may need a redacted instruction with a
lower authority ceiling than the source. A reader edition may need a paraphrase
that is useful for orientation but useless as evidence.

All of those can derive from the same source. They should not inherit the same
meaning.

Without typed cells and representation certificates, compact context drifts.
A planning summary later sounds like evidence. A redacted instruction is
mistaken for the full source. A paraphrase from a reader edition becomes a
supporting citation. A quote stripped of its clearance label is sent to a
worker that was never allowed to see it. The error is not only that something
was summarized. The error is that the summary lost the contract around what it
was allowed to do.

## Why Existing Approaches Are Insufficient

Summaries can look authoritative while dropping source bindings, omissions,
authority ceilings, or allowed-use limits.

Retrieval makes relevant material available. Long-context models make larger
material visible. Memory systems make prior state reusable. None of that is
enough if the derived representation does not carry what changed during
compression. A summary may preserve the main point while dropping dissent,
chronology, uncertainty, exact wording, source revocation, or a limit on who
may use it. That kind of loss is easy to miss because the output still reads
fluently.

The external context literature supplies useful pressure without proving this
certificate scheme. RAG makes retrieved evidence explicit. Memory-operating
approaches show why context needs policy. Long-context evaluations and
position-sensitivity work show that fitting text into a window is not the same
as reliably using it. Self-retrieval and citation-support work make the
boundary between generation and evidence visible. Prompt-compression work
shows why compressed representations need loss accounting.

Semantic pages do not claim to beat those systems here. They add a contract
around the derived object. The question becomes not "is this text relevant?"
but "what kind of cell is this, which source binds it, what did it drop, who
may use it, and what may it support?"

## Core Claim

VCM should represent context as typed semantic pages or cells with
evidence-carrying representation certificates. This remains an architectural
argument.

The support is bounded. VCM public v1 supplies semantic objects,
representations, validations, packets, materializations, source and authority
binding, protected compilation, adequacy/admission separation, revocation,
invalidation, and audit records. Verification Bandwidth explains why lossy
summaries reduce verification capacity unless loss is visible. Spinoza
supplies claim/evidence-cell pressure through proof-, citation-, and
procedure-carrying claim graphs. Context Engineer supplies structured mission
briefs, clearance fields, context shards, and need-to-know delivery. Editable
VCM is useful terminology and future citation-triage context, not local
certificate-system evidence.

The live claim therefore stays at `argument` support. The repository has a
schema/fixture surface, finite Lean predicates, and a synthetic
context-admission/adequacy harness. It does not have a semantic
summary-fidelity evaluator, certificate truthfulness checker,
omission-completeness audit, open-domain autoformalization result,
benchmark reproduction, or model-facing certificate result.

## Mechanism

The mechanism is a representation receipt. A source becomes one or more cells,
and every derived cell carries a certificate.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Source cell"] --> B["Representation choice"]
  B --> C["Exact excerpt"]
  B --> D["Lossy planning summary"]
  B --> E["Redacted execution packet"]
  B --> F["Claim or contradiction cell"]
  C --> G["Semantic page certificate"]
  D --> G
  E --> G
  F --> G
  G --> H{"Use allowed?"}
  H -- "yes" --> I["Packet, plan, claim ledger, or artifact"]
  H -- "no" --> J["Typed refusal or residual"]
```

The certificate records the type of representation. Exact carriage, lossy
summary, redaction, abstraction, translation, and derived inference have
different rights and different risks. Calling them all "context" loses the
reason the certificate exists.

The certificate also records the source bindings, derivation links,
transaction references, artifact references, omissions, loss contract,
authority ceiling, permitted uses, validity window, verification state,
revocation state, residual risks, and non-claims. Those fields are not
ceremonial. They determine whether a cell can be used for planning, citation,
execution, proof support, contradiction checking, reader orientation, or none
of the above.

The important move is that one source can produce several honest
representations. The exact excerpt can support quotation. The planning summary
can help a scheduler choose work. The redacted execution packet can be shown to
a worker without leaking source material. The contradiction cell can be routed
to a claim ledger. The reader paraphrase can help a human understand the idea.
Each cell may be useful, but each is useful under a different contract.

## Interfaces

Semantic pages sit at the boundary between memory, planning, reasoning, and
execution.

Planning consumes constraint cells, decision cells, and planning summaries.
Spinoza-style claim ledgers consume claim/evidence cells, citation links,
proof artifacts, procedure records, contradiction records, defeaters, and
revision history. Execution receives only representations whose certificate
allows the action and authority context. Artifact graphs consume certificate
references when a derived representation becomes part of durable work.

A practical semantic page certificate needs at least these fields:

- `page_id`
- `cell_type`
- `source_bindings`
- `derived_from`
- `transaction_refs`
- `artifact_refs`
- `representation_kind`
- `loss_contract`
- `omissions`
- `authority_ceiling`
- `permitted_uses`
- `validity_window`
- `verification_state`
- `revocation_state`
- `residual_risks`
- `non_claims`

Those fields prevent a cell from silently changing role. A planning summary
does not become a citation because it appears in a claim discussion. A
reader-edition paraphrase does not become proof support because it reads more
cleanly than the source. An exact excerpt does not become executable
permission because it contains an instruction. A redacted packet does not
become a full source because it came from one.

## Invariants

Authority labels survive summarization.

Loss contracts are explicit.

A derived cell points back to source bindings.

A derived cell points back to the transaction or artifact record that created
the representation when that record exists.

Authority non-escalation is the most important invariant. A summary cannot
become more citable, more executable, more secret-cleared, or more verified
than the source and certificate allow. Moving a cell into a plan, claim
ledger, job, report, or reader edition cannot broaden its permitted use. If
the target use falls outside the certificate, the system needs a stronger
representation, a new review, or a refusal.

The current Lean hooks are narrow. They prove finite-record properties: a
valid derived context cell carries source bindings plus loss/use declarations,
and a summary that respects source authority cannot raise its authority
ceiling above the source cells. They do not prove that the summary is faithful,
that omissions are complete, or that the certificate truthfully describes the
source.

## Failure Modes

Summary overconfidence is the everyday failure. A short, fluent cell sounds
complete, so a reader or model treats it as if it preserved every relevant
constraint.

Provenance loss is the traceability failure. The cell keeps the gist but loses
the source binding needed to audit, quote, challenge, or revise it.

Authority escalation via compression is the governance failure. A source with
a narrow authority label is compressed into a harmless-looking note and then
used in a setting where the source itself would have been denied.

Certificate laundering is subtler. The cell has a certificate, so downstream
layers stop asking whether the certificate is current, truthful, and adequate
for the new use. The certificate should make review easier. It should not make
review optional.

Stale-cell reuse is the lifecycle failure. A source is corrected, revoked, or
tainted, but an older derived cell remains in circulation as if nothing
changed.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The smallest honest implementation is a semantic-page certificate schema,
valid and invalid fixtures, finite proof hooks, and a synthetic cross-record
harness that blocks stale, lossy, or authority-escalating use.

The repository has that narrow surface. The semantic page certificate fixture
validates record shape: source bindings, derivation, transaction references,
artifact references, representation kind, loss contract, omissions, authority
ceiling, permitted uses, validity, verification state, revocation state,
residual risks, and non-claims. `AsiStackProofs.ContextCertificates` proves
finite-record certificate completeness and authority non-escalation
properties. The context admission/adequacy harness checks synthetic cross-record
consistency for context ABI records, context packets, semantic page
certificates, context transactions, and adequacy records. Its recorded local
run passed three valid fixtures and rejected five expected-invalid fixtures.

That is evidence for record discipline. It is not evidence that summaries are
semantically faithful. It does not prove omission completeness, certificate
truthfulness, source interpretation, open-domain claim formalization,
contradiction-rate reduction, leak prevention, or model-facing context quality.

The next useful slice is a paired source/derived-cell fixture set: one source
excerpt, one exact evidence cell, one lossy planning summary with omissions
declared, one redacted execution cell with a lower authority ceiling, one
reader paraphrase marked orientation-only, one stale certificate invalidated by
source revocation, and one attempted use outside the certificate blocked. That
would make the certificate semantics testable without pretending to solve
natural-language summary fidelity.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature version is a representation-certification system for context.
It does not merely summarize sources. It turns raw sources, exact excerpts,
planning briefs, redacted instructions, claim cells, contradiction pairs, and
derived inferences into typed objects whose permitted use, loss, authority,
and provenance remain attached wherever the object travels.

At that maturity, a model does not receive "a summary." It receives a
certificate-bearing cell. The certificate says whether the cell is exact or
lossy, which source and transaction created it, which artifact carries it,
what was omitted, what authority ceiling survived, whether it may support a
claim, whether it may drive execution, whether it is adequate for the current
target, whether it has expired, and what residual remains.

The result is not anti-compression. It is governed compression. The system can
make context smaller, safer, more task-relative, and more readable while still
preserving the liabilities of the source. A good certificate makes a compact
cell harder to misuse as it travels.

That endpoint is not implemented here. It needs paired source/derived-cell
fixtures, certificate truthfulness tests, summary-fidelity tests, omission
audits, stale/revoked-cell conformance checks, downstream use gates, and
model-facing evaluation before it can support stronger claims.

## What Is Proven, And What Is Not

The current project proves and tests record-level discipline.

It checks certificate shape. It checks finite derived-cell completeness. It
checks finite authority non-escalation for summary cells. It checks synthetic
cross-record behavior where stale certificates, weak verification modes,
conflicts, propagated taint, and open deletion obligations cannot become
evidence-ready support.

It does not prove the truth of a certificate. It does not prove that a summary
preserves all relevant meaning. It does not detect every omission. It does not
prove that a claim cell captures the correct formalization. It does not run a
VCM-Bench result, reproduce Context Engineer benchmark figures, validate a
deployed Digital SCIF, or show that model outputs improve when using certified
cells.

The boundary matters because certificates are powerful notation. They make a
representation auditable. They do not make it true.

## Summary

Semantic pages and context cells make context units typed. Certificates keep
those units from losing their obligations as they move through the stack.

The rule is not "never summarize." The rule is "do not let a summary forget
what it dropped." A governed system can use exact excerpts, planning briefs,
redacted packets, claim cells, contradiction pairs, and reader paraphrases, but
each representation has to carry its provenance, loss, authority, permitted
use, validity, revocation state, and residual risk.

The next layer turns those certified cells into a changing memory state.
Context Transactions, Snapshots, Mounts, and Taint asks how context updates,
branches, deletion obligations, taint, and stale views become auditable events
instead of silent edits.
